source_label: Bitcoin Core — Running A Full Node
excerpt: Bitcoin Core can be configured to run as a pruned node, retaining only enough block data to maintain the UTXO set. Pruned operation reduces disk requirements to roughly 10 GB while preserving full consensus enforcement. Initial block download remains a function of CPU and bandwidth and typically takes between one and three days on consumer hardware.
body:
Source excerpt:
Bitcoin Core can be configured to run as a pruned node, retaining only enough block data to maintain the UTXO set. Pruned operation reduces disk requirements to roughly 10 GB while preserving full consensus enforcement. Initial block download remains a function of CPU and bandwidth and typically takes between one and three days on consumer hardware.

---

Cited by leaf "Running your own full node":
A full node downloads every block since the genesis block, validates every transaction in every block against the consensus rules, and keeps a current view of the UTXO set. What this gets you operationally: your wallet queries your own node for balance and history, you broadcast transactions to your own peer-to-peer network connection, and you don't have to trust any third party's view of "what is the current state of Bitcoin" [1]. The cost is some disk and a one-time multi-day initial sync.

Resource requirements as of 2026: ~700 GB of disk if you keep the full chain, ~10 GB if you run pruned (which keeps consensus enforcement but discards old block data), 4–8 GB of RAM, and a sustained internet connection that can handle the upload bandwidth peers will draw from you (a few hundred GB/month is typical). Initial block download is the painful part — it takes one to several days on consumer hardware depending on CPU, disk, and which validation shortcuts (assumeutxo, assumevalid) you accept [2].

What a full node does not do: it does not by itself give you privacy (your wallet still has to query the node in a way that doesn't leak your addresses to your ISP); it does not pay you for being run; it does not give you a vote in any governance process. What it does do, in addition to the trust-minimized wallet experience, is contribute to the network's resilience by serving blocks to other peers and by being one more independent enforcement point for the consensus rules [3]. The honest framing: running a node is an operational discipline, not a hobby with a payoff curve. The payoff is a different relationship to your own money.
